days.sitting_overview: The Commons sitting on 2 June 2026 was dominated by a statement on the murder of 18-year-old Henry Nowak, in which the Home Secretary committed to a fully resourced Independent Office for Police Conduct investigation, rejected calls to review the kirpan religious exemption, and refused to frame the case as one of preferential treatment of communities. An urgent question on the Milburn interim report on young people not in education, employment or training, departmental questions to the Energy Secretary set against the war in Iran and a 13 per cent price cap rise, the Committee stage of the Armed Forces Bill on housing, justice and reserves, and an adjournment debate on systemic Department for Work and Pensions errors affecting Scottish carers filled the rest of the day.

Industrial and economic policy ran through the day: the Chancellor set out a package of measures in response to rising costs, with funds for the chemicals and ceramics industries, and the Commons gave a Second Reading to the Bill that would bring British Steel into public ownership. The Foreign Office also made statements on Iran, the Strait of Hormuz and the wider region.

An urgent question on Government licences permitting imports of diesel and jet fuel refined from Russian crude drew an apology from the Trade Minister. Oral questions to the Technology Secretary covered the protection of children online, and the fifth day of the King's Speech debate, on defence, included Wes Streeting's first Commons speech since he resigned from the Cabinet.

The Transport Secretary set out a reset of HS2, putting the cost of completion at £87.7 billion to £102.7 billion and the first London to Birmingham services between 2036 and 2039. An urgent question saw the Intelligence and Security Committee accuse the Government of redacting and withholding documents beyond the terms of the Mandelson Humble Address, and the fourth day of the King's Speech debate, on energy security, divided the House over the Bill to end new North Sea oil and gas licences.
